article_title: Thousands of consumer routers hacked by Russia's military
core_thesis: The Russian military intelligence group APT28 (Forest Blizzard) has compromised an estimated 18,000–40,000 consumer routers (primarily MikroTik and TP-Link) across 120 countries by exploiting unpatched vulnerabilities in end-of-life devices. The attackers altered DNS settings to redirect traffic from targeted government and enterprise domains to adversary-in-the-middle servers, capturing OAuth tokens and credentials even after multifactor authentication. This campaign demonstrates APT28's blend of sophisticated tools (e.g., LLM 'LAMEHUG') and classic techniques, and highlights the persistent risk posed by unpatched consumer-grade routers in espionage operations.
detailed_summary: Researchers from Lumen Technologies' Black Lotus Labs reported that APT28, a Russian GRU-linked advanced persistent threat group, hacked thousands of consumer routers (mostly MikroTik and TP-Link) in 120 countries starting May 2025. The operation initially targeted a limited number of devices, but escalated after Britain's NCSC published an alert in August 2025 about a related credential-harvesting campaign. Over a four-week period from December 12, 2025, Black Lotus observed over 290,000 distinct IP addresses sending DNS requests to malicious APT28 resolvers. The attackers exploited known vulnerabilities in end-of-life routers that lacked security patches, then changed DNS settings via DHCP to redirect traffic for select authentication-related domains (e.g., Microsoft 365) to proxy servers acting as adversary-in-the-middle (AitM). These servers used self-signed TLS certificates; if users ignored browser warnings, the attackers captured OAuth tokens and credentials after the user completed multifactor authentication. The routers were used as proxies to reach a smaller number of high-value targets, including foreign ministries and law enforcement agencies. APT28 has a history of router hacking, including the 2018 VPNFilter campaign affecting 500,000 devices and a 2024 operation disrupted by the US Justice Department. The article advises users to check DNS settings for unrecognized servers, review event logs for unauthorized DNS changes, replace end-of-life routers, and never click through TLS certificate warnings.

entities.name: APT28
entities.role: Russian military intelligence threat group (GRU) behind the router hacking campaign

entities.name: Black Lotus Labs
entities.role: Research division of Lumen Technologies that discovered and reported the campaign

entities.name: MikroTik
entities.role: Router manufacturer; many of its devices were compromised

entities.name: TP-Link
entities.role: Router manufacturer; many of its devices were compromised

entities.name: GRU
entities.role: Russian military intelligence agency that controls APT28

entities.name: NCSC (UK)
entities.role: Britain's National Cyber Security Center; released an alert that triggered escalation of the campaign

entities.name: Microsoft
entities.role: Confirmed that domains for Microsoft 365 were targeted in the DNS hijacking

entities.name: Dan Goodin
entities.role: Senior Security Editor at Ars Technica and author of the article

key_data_points: An estimated 18,000–40,000 consumer routers (mostly MikroTik and TP-Link) were compromised across 120 countries (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates scale of router-based botnets for credential harvesting).
APT28 used end-of-life routers with unpatched known vulnerabilities as initial access vectors (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: highlights importance of scanning for outdated firmware in target networks).

The attackers changed DNS settings via DHCP to redirect traffic for targeted authentication domains (e.g., Microsoft 365) to adversary-in-the-middle proxy servers (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this technique can be replicated for phishing or token theft).

The AitM servers used self-signed TLS certificates; if users ignored browser warnings, the attackers captured OAuth tokens and credentials even after MFA completion (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates how to bypass MFA via token theft).

Over a four-week period starting December 12, 2025, more than 290,000 distinct IP addresses sent DNS requests to the malicious APT28 resolver (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: indicates large-scale DNS redirection infrastructure).

The campaign escalated rapidly after the UK NCSC published an alert in August 2025 about a related credential-harvesting campaign (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: shows how threat actors adapt to public disclosures).
APT28 has a history of router hacking, including the 2018 VPNFilter campaign (500,000 devices) and a 2024 operation (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: confirms persistent use of router compromise as a TTP).
methodology_steps: Identify end-of-life consumer routers (e.g., MikroTik, TP-Link) with known, unpatched vulnerabilities.
Exploit those vulnerabilities to gain administrative access to the routers.
Change the DNS settings on the compromised routers to point to malicious DNS resolvers controlled by the attacker.
Propagate the malicious DNS settings to workstations on the local LAN via DHCP.
Configure the malicious DNS resolver to return the attacker's proxy server IP address for targeted authentication domains (e.g., Microsoft 365), while passing all other traffic normally.
Set up an adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) proxy server that presents a self-signed TLS certificate to the end user.
When a user visits a targeted domain and ignores the TLS warning, the proxy captures all traffic, including OAuth tokens and credentials after MFA is completed.
Use the compromised routers as proxies to reach high-value targets (e.g., foreign ministries, law enforcement) for espionage.
conclusions: APT28's campaign demonstrates that consumer-grade routers, especially end-of-life models, remain a weak link in network security and are actively exploited for large-scale credential theft and espionage. The use of DNS hijacking combined with AitM proxies allows attackers to bypass MFA by stealing OAuth tokens. Users and organizations should replace unsupported routers, monitor DNS settings for unauthorized changes, and never ignore TLS certificate warnings. For Red Teamers, this campaign provides a blueprint for simulating realistic adversary-in-the-middle attacks using compromised network devices.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests as it details a real-world, large-scale operation that combines router exploitation, DNS hijacking, and adversary-in-the-middle techniques to steal credentials and bypass MFA. You can apply these TTPs in penetration testing engagements to simulate advanced persistent threats. The technical specifics—such as exploiting unpatched router vulnerabilities, manipulating DHCP/DNS, and capturing OAuth tokens—are directly actionable for building offensive security labs or homelab exercises. Additionally, the article underscores the importance of maintaining updated firmware and monitoring network configurations, which aligns with your homelab and DevOps focus on secure infrastructure.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a lab environment with old MikroTik or TP-Link routers to practice the exact exploitation and DNS hijacking techniques described. You could also develop a detection script that monitors DNS server changes via DHCP logs and alerts on unrecognized resolvers—this would be a valuable addition to your homelab's security monitoring stack.
